translations.translation:
Yu Jin (Wenze)
于禁 (文則)
Lived AD ?–221
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 17 (Wei 17)
byZL181
onMarch 14th, 2025

AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'created_at' changed to '1741535342' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
Yu Jin, styledWenze, was fromJuping[鉅平],Taishan[泰山].
When theYellow Scarvesrebelled,Bao Xinrecruited and gathered soldiers.[Yu] Jinwas among the followers.
When theGrand ProgenitorledYan province[兗州],[Yu] Jinand his comrades all went to him, and they served as lieutenants of one hundred soldiers, being subordinate to GeneralWang Lang.[Wang] Langsaw him as extraordinary and recommended[Yu] Jinwas befitting a Grand General in talent. When theGrand Progenitorsummoned, met, and talked with him, he conferred him to be ArmyMajorand had him led troops toXu province[徐州], attackingGuangqi[廣威] and capturing it. He was conferred asCommandant who Breaks Formations. He followed to suppressLü BuatPuyang[濮陽], detaching and defeating two camps of[Lü] Busouth of the county, then separated again to defeatGao YaatXuchang[須昌]. He also followed to attackShouzhang[壽張],Dingtao[定陶], andLihu[離狐], and he besiegedZhang ChaoatYongqiu[雍丘], and all was captured. He followed to conquer theYellow ScarvesLiu PiandHuang Shao, among others, garrisoning at Banliang.1[Huang] Shaoand the others struck the camp of theGrand Progenitorover the night, but[Yu] Jinled his subordinates to strike and defeat them, beheading[Liu] Pi2and[Huang] Shao, and the rest of their troops surrendered. He was promoted toColonel who Pacifies Caitiffs. He followed to besiegeQiao RuiatKu[苦], beheading[Qiao] Ruiand others, four generals intotal.•••
Liu PiandHuang Shaoare stated to have been active in theRunanandYingchuanareas. I could not find a county by the name of Banliang [版梁] in them, and I suspect that it means something else. I had attempted to look for any meaning if it was not a place name, and I came up with a few thoughts. 版 can mean “to build walls with wood plates” and 梁 can mean “to build bridges”. 梁 can also refer toLiang, which was in the same province asRunanandYingchuan. The sentence can thus either be read as “garrisoning, and they built wooden walls and bridges” or “garrisoning, building wooden walls atLiang". However, I am unsure if this is correct, and so I kept the vague Banliang to reduce confusion.↩
Some versions of the Chinese text omit writing down this instance of "[Liu] Pi”, likely because he was not mentioned as being part of the night raid byHuang Shao.↩
He followed to arrive atYuan[宛], getting the surrender ofZhang Xiu, but when[Zhang] Xiurebelled, theGrand Progenitorwas at a disadvantage in fighting with him, and the army was defeated and withdrew toWuyin[舞陰]. At the time, the army was in disarray and they each travelled on sparse roads in search of theGrand Progenitor. Only the hundreds of soldiers led by[Yu] Jinwere fighting and withdrawing simultaneously, and even though there were some who died or got injured, they did not separate from each other. When the pursuit of the enemies slowed down a bit,[Yu] Jinslowly organized the units, beating the drums in retreat. He had not yet arrived to where theGrand Progenitorwas when he saw over ten people on the road who were wounded and naked that were running.[Yu] Jinasked what happened, and they said, “We were robbed by theQing province[靑州] soldiers.” Previously, when theYellow Scarvessurrendered, they were titled theQing province[靑州] soldiers and theGrand Progenitorshowed them leniency. Thus, they dared to loot.[Yu] Jinwas furious and ordered his troops, “TheQing province[靑州] soldiers were all subordinate toExcellency Cao, yet they have gone back to being brigands!” He then suppressed them and berated them for their crimes. TheQing province[靑州] soldiers hurried to theGrand Progenitorand slandered him. When[Yu] Jinarrived, he first set up camps and walls; he did not visit theGrand Progenitorat that moment. Someone told[Yu] Jin, “TheQing province[靑州] soldiers have already slandered you, sir. You should hurry toHis Excellencyto make your case.”[Yu] Jinsaid, “The enemies are at our rear right now, and they can catch up in no time. If we do not prepare beforehand, how could we handle the enemy? Moreover,His Excellencyis intelligent and wise. Would he believe mere slander?” He slowly dug a moat and set up camp, and once he was done, he then entered to visit, explaining the situation. TheGrand Progenitorwas pleased and told[Yu] Jin, “I was desperate during the predicament ofYu River[淯水],3but you,General, were able to organize amidst disarray, suppress violence, and build strong walls. That is such unwavering integrity! Even with the famed generals of old, how could that be surpassed?” Hence, he considered all the merits[Yu] Jinrendered and enfeoffed him asDistrict Marquisof Yishou [益壽亭侯]. Later, he followed to attackZhang Xiuagain atRang[穰], and he capturedLü BuatXiapi[下邳]. He then separated withShi HuanandCao Rento attackSui GuatShequan[射犬], defeating and beheadinghim.•••
Referring to the battle withZhang Xiu.↩
When theGrand Progenitorfirst invadedYuan Shao, the army of[Yuan] Shaowas mighty, yet[Yu] Jinwished to be the vanguard. TheGrand Progenitoradmired this and when he sent two thousand infantry, he tasked[Yu] Jinwith leading them to guardYan ford[延津] to resist[Yuan] Shao. TheGrand Progenitorwithdrew the army back toGuandu[官渡], and whenLiu Beirevolted withXu province[徐州], theGrand Progenitorwent on an eastern expedition against him.[Yuan] Shaoattacked[Yu] Jin, but[Yu] Jinmaintained a firm defence, and so[Yuan] Shaowas unable to make gains. He andYue Jin, among others, led five thousand infantry and cavalry to strike a camp of[Yuan] Shao, then went southwest along theYellow RiverfromYan ford[延津] to the two counties ofJi[汲] andHuojia[獲嘉], burning over thirty defending garrisons. They each beheaded or captured alive in the thousands, and over twenty generals of[Yuan] Shao, among themHe MaoandWang Mo, surrendered. TheGrand Progenitoragain sent[Yu] Jinto separate and garrisonYuanwu[原武], He struck a camp of[Yuan] ShaoatDushi ford[杜氏津] and defeated them. He was promoted toLieutenant General. Later, he followed back toGuandu[官渡]. TheGrand Progenitorand[Yuan] Shaoset up continuous camps and they built earth hills to face off against each other.[Yuan] Shaoshot into the camp, and many soldiers died or got wounded, causing the army to be in fright.[Yu] Jinsupervised and guarded his earth hill, fighting with all of his strength and his spirit becoming even more energetic.[Yuan] Shaowas defeated, and he was promoted toDeputy General.Ji province[冀州] was pacified.
WhenChang Xirebelled once more,[Yu] Jinwas sent to conquer him.[Yu] Jinquickly moved forward to attack[Chang] Xi, but as[Chang] Xiwas old friends with[Yu] Jin, he surrendered to[Yu] Jin. The generals all believed that as[Chang] Xihad already surrendered, he should be delivered to theGrand Progenitor, but[Yu] Jinsaid, “Do you gentlemen not know of the enduring command ofHis Excellency? That those who surrender after being besieged are not to be shown mercy? To undertake the law and execute commands is the integrity of serving your superior. Even though[Chang] Xiis my old friend, how can I lose that integrity?” He personally bid farewell4to[Chang] Xi, shed tears, and beheaded him. At the time, the army of theGrand Progenitorwas atChunyu[淳于], and when he heard about this, he sighed and said, “For[Chang] Xito surrender not to me but to[Yu] Jin, how could that have not been his fate?” He valued[Yu] Jineven more.ADonghai[東海] was pacified and[Yu] Jinwas conferred asGeneral of Tiger Might.•••
決 is translated as “bid farewell” here, but the character itself also means “to judge” and “to decide”, perhaps in reference toYu Jindeciding the fate ofChang Xi.↩
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, would comment on this. Even though the law would not show mercy to those who surrender after being besieged, to imprison and deliver them would not constitute a violation of the command.[Yu] Jinnever once hoped for that one in ten thousand5for his old friend, but instead indulged in a bloodthirsty heart, thereby opposing the sentiment of the masses. Hence, for him to become a prisoner who surrendered in the end and to be given a nasty posthumous title after his death is quiteproper!•••
The possibility thatChang Xiwould be spared.↩
Later, he andZang Ba, among others, attackedMei ChengwhileZhang LiaoandZhang He, among others, suppressedChen Lan. When[Yu] Jinarrived,[Mei] Chenghad his force of over three thousand strong surrender. After he surrendered, he rebelled again, and had his troops rush towards[Chen] Lan.[Zhang] Liaoand the others were in a stalemate with[Chen] Lanand their military provisions were few.[Yu] Jindelivered provisions continuously, and so[Zhang] Liaobeheaded[Chen] Lanand[Mei] Cheng. His fief was increased by two hundred, joining with the previous for a total of one thousand and two hundred households.
At the time,[Yu] Jin,Zhang Liao,Yue Jin,Zhang He, andXu Huangwere all famed generals. Every time theGrand Progenitorwent out on expeditions and conquests, all of them would rotate with each other in serving as the army vanguard, then move back into being as the defender at the rear.[Yu] Jinled his army with sternness and order, and whenever he seized wealth and goods from traitors, he would not privately take any of them. Hence, the rewards and gifts he got were many. However, he used the law in controlling his subordinates, so he was not able to gain the hearts of the soldiers.
TheGrand Progenitorhad long resentedZhu Lingand wanted to seize his barracks. Because[Yu] Jinhad immense prestige,[Yu] Jinwas sent to led tens of cavalry to present the command and directly went to the barracks of[Zhu] Lingto seize his army.[Zhu] Lingand his subordinates did not dare to act. Thus,[Zhu] Lingwas made a ranking subordinate under[Yu] Jin. The soldiers were all shaken up; such was how he was dreaded. He was promoted toDocument Scribeand given acting tally and battle-axe. Five hundred households was divided from his fief to enfeoff one of his sons as a ranged marquis.
In the twenty-fourth year of theera [219], theGrand Progenitorwas atChang’an[長安], and he sentCao Rento suppressGuan YuatFan[樊], and he also sent[Yu] Jinto aid[Cao] Ren. During autumn, there was pouring rain, causing theHan River[漢水] to overflow, with several yards of water over the flat earth. The seven armies of[Yu] Jinand some others were all submerged.[Yu] Jinand the other generals went on high [terrain] to observe the water, but there was nowhere they could escape to.[Guan] Yuwas on a large boat and went to attack[Yu] Jinand the others.[Yu] Jinthus surrendered. It was onlyPang Dewho did not curb his integrity, instead dying. When theGrand Progenitorheard about this, he let out a very long sad sigh, then said, “I have known[Yu] Jinfor thirty years! How could I anticipate that when he was faced with danger and placed in a predicament, he would instead not compare toPang De!” It happened thatSun Quancaptured[Guan] Yuand seized his troops, so[Yu] Jinresided inWu.
When theCivil Emperorascended the throne steps,[Sun] Quandeclared himself to be a vassal and sent[Yu] Jinback. TheEmperorbrought in[Yu] Jinfor a meeting, whose beard and hair was shining white, and whose appearance was gaunt and haggard. He wept tears and kowtowed, but theEmperorcomforted him by citing the former matters of Xun Linfu [荀林父]6and Mengming Shi [孟明視]7Band conferred him asGeneral who Secures the Distant. He wanted to send him as an envoy toWu, but first ordered him to go north toYe[鄴] to visitSupremeMausoleum [高陵]. TheEmperorhad instructed beforehand for the rooms of the mausoleum to be painted with the depiction ofGuan Yutriumphing in battle,Pang Debeing enraged, and[Yu] Jinsurrendering. When[Yu] Jinsaw this, he felt so ashamed and enraged that he fell ill anddied.•••
Xun Linfu was a general of the Jin state. He suffered a large defeat against Chu forces, and was to be executed. However, he was spared. Xun Linfu learned from his mistakes and became a better general.↩
Baili Shi [百里視], styled Mengming [孟明], was a general of the Qin state. He was once defeated and captured by Jin forces, but when he was released, the Duke of Qin forgave him. He would eventually achieve victories later on.↩
TheBook of Weicontains the command, which stated:
“Of old, Xun Linfu suffered a great loss at Bi [邲] and Mengming was defeated with his army at Xiao [殽].QinandJindid not substitute them, but restored them to their ranks. After this,Jinseized the lands of the Di barbarians andQinheld hegemony over the western Rong barbarians. If even these tiny and small nations still valued them in such manners, what more is there to say of a large empire? The defeat atFancheng[樊城] was due to the flood rising up violently, not some fault in fighting.[Yu] Jinand the others shall be restored to their offices.”
His son,[Yu] Gui, succeeded him, and was enfeoffed asDistrict Marquisof Yishou [益壽亭侯].[Yu] Jinwas posthumously titled theSevereMarquis[厲侯].

Yu Jin (Wenze)

AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'created_at' changed to '1742616326' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
Yu Jin, styled Wenze, is from the Tai Mountain prefecture at the Ju Ping County. During the Yellow Turban rebellion, Bao Xin gathered the people to fight against the threat. Yu Jin initially wanted to serve under him. However, when Cao Cao took over Jizhou, Yu Jin and his friend went to serve him. He was placed under Wang Lang as Du Po. Wang Lang treated Yu Jin with the greatest respect and recommended him for the position of a general. After Cao Cao met Yu Jin for a talk, he appointed the latter as a Jun Si Ma. Cao Cao then ordered Yu Jin to march to Xuzhou to attack Guangwei. He was then promoted to Xian Chen Du Wei.
Yu Jin went with Cao Cao to Pu Yang to attack Lu Bu. He defeated two camps of troops under Lu Bu. He also scored a major victory against Gao Ya. He later followed Cao Cao to attack Shou Zhang, Ting Tao, Li Gu and other areas. He surrounded Zhang Chao at Yangqiu and returned triumphant. Yu Jin went with Cao Cao to attack the Yellow Turbans, Liu Pi and Huang Shao. He stationed his troops at Pan Liang. Huang Shao and the rest attempted an ambush on Cao Cao during the night. However, Yu Jin rallied his troops to fight off the attackers and defeated them. He killed Huang Shao and his people while managing to capture all their troops. As a result, he was promoted to Ping Lu Jiao Wei.
Later, Yu Jin went with Cao Cao to Ku County to surround Qiao Rui. He killed four of Qiao Rui’s generals. Following this, he followed Cao Cao to Wan to get Zhang Xiu to surrender. However, Zhang Xiu unexpectedly rebelled and Cao Cao was repeatedly defeated in battle. The army then retreated to Wu Yin. At that time, the army was in a total chaos. Everyone was trying to escape back to Cao Cao’s military base using the backroads. Only Yu Jin continued to fight while the army withdrew. His troops retreated in an orderly fashion without scattering. Eventually, when the enemy troops halted their pursuit, Yu Jin was able to relax and to reorganize his troop formation. After that, he sounded the drums to order for the return to base. However, on his journey back, Yu Jin encountered a score of people with their clothes completely stripped from them. They were also injured. When Yu Jin queried them about the matter, they replied, “We were robbed by the soldiers of Qingzhou and we were also injured by them.”
When the Yellow Turbans surrendered earlier on, they called themselves the soldiers of Qingzhou. As Cao Cao was too lenient with them, they started to get bold and began to cause trouble. They also committed robbery in a brazen and open manner. Yu Jin became very angry after he learn about the matter and said to his men, “Although the Qingzhou soldiers also serve under Lord Cao together with us, they dare to cause trouble by committing banditry.”
He then led his men to attack the Qingzhou soldiers and to denounce their crimes. The Qingzhou soldiers immediately ran to Cao Cao to make accusations against Yu Jin. When Yu Jin returned to base, he did not see Cao Cao immediately. He first ordered his troops to set up camp.
Someone then advised Yu Jin, “The Qingzhou soldiers had already said bad things about you in front of Lord Cao. You should clarify the matter immediately with him.”
Yu Jin replied, “The enemy soldiers are right behind us. They could catch up at anytime. If we do not prepare to face them first, how can we deal with them later on? Anyway, our lord is discriminating and he will not believe their lies.”
He continued to supervise his troops to set up camp and to dig trenches. When it was settled, only did he went to meet Cao Cao and reported the matter to him. Cao Cao was very happy after he heard the matter and said to Yu Jin, “The trouble at Yu Shui is my direst hour. You have managed to control your troops amidst such chaos to fight off these enemies and to hold the position. You have shown the virtue of loyalty and steadfastness. Even the generals of ancient times are no match for you.”
Thus, Cao Cao recorded Yu Jin’s achievements from the past and the present and appointed the latter as the Marquis of Continuous Longevity.
Yu Jin followed Cao Cao to Rang County to attack Zhang Xiu and then to Xia Pi to capture Lu Bu. Furthermore, he also attacked Gui Gu at Si Quan together with Shi Huan and Cao Ren. He achieved a great victory and killed Gui Gu.
When Cao Cao began his campaign against Yuan Shao, the latter’s military power was very powerful. Yu Jin proclaimed his bravery and volunteered to become the vanguard. Cao Cao greatly praised Yu Jin and assigned him to defend against Yuan Shao at Yan Jin. Cao Cao himself returned with his troops to Guan Du. When Liu Bei created an uprising in Xuzhou, Cao Cao went to put it down. Yuan Shao took advantage of this to attack Yu Jin. Yu Jin defended his position fiercely and Yuan Shao was unable to prevail. Moreover, Yu Jin together with Yue Jin led 5,000 infantry and cavalry troops to attack Yuan Shao’s military camps nearby – starting from the southwest of Yan Jin, along the Yellow River until the two counties of Ji and Hu Jia. They burned a total of over 30 fortifications while killing and capturing thousands of men. They also caused two of Yuan Shao’s great generals, He Mao and Wang Mo, to surrender.
Cao Cao then ordered Yu Jin to station his troops at Yuan Wu to attack Yuan Shao’s military bases near Du Shi Jun. He also achieved victory in the matter. He was then promoted to Major-General. After that, he followed Cao Cao back to Guan Du. The camps of both Cao Cao and Yuan Shao were very close to each other. Both sides had built a mound of earth to oppose each other. Yuan Shao attacked Cao Cao’s camps with arrows and killed a lot of his soldiers. The soldiers became very afraid as a result. Yu Jin supervised the troops to defend the mound and gave fierce resistance to Yuan Shao’s troops. He managed to increase the momentum and he finally defeated Yuan Shao. He was promoted as Lieutenant-General.
When Yizhou was pacified, Cheng Xi started a rebellion. Cao Cao sent Yu Jin to put down the rebellion again. Yu Jin rushed to attack Cheng Xi and the latter was unable to prevail. As Cheng Xi and Yu Jin were friends, the former decided to surrender to Yu Jin. All of the generals feel that Cheng Xi should be sent to Cao Cao to be dealt with as he had already surrendered. Yu Jin said, “Have you forgotten what Lord Cao usually exhorts us to do? A man who surrenders after he is surrounded cannot be pardoned. Carrying out the law is the duty of a vassal in obeying his lord. Although Cheng Xi is my old friend, how can I forget my duty as a vassal?”
He personally visited Cheng Xi to bid farewell. He wept whiled he had Cheng Xi executed. At that time, Cao Cao was stationed at Chun Yu. When he heard news about the matter, he sighed and said, “Cheng Xi wanted to surrender but he doesn’t come to me. Instead he went to Yu Jin. Isn’t his death fated?”
From then on, Cao Cao made even greater use of Yu Jin. He appointed Yu Jin to become the General of Tiger’s Might after the latter pacified Tong Hai.
Later on, Yu Jin attacked Mei Cheng together with Zang Ba. Zhang Liao, Zhang He and the rest was sent on a punitive expedition on Chen Lan. When Yu Jin arrived, Mei Cheng surrendered to him with his 3,000 men. However, he revolted again later and brought his men to seek refuge with Chen Lan. Zhang Liao and the others could not prevail for long against Chen Lan as their provisions were dwindling. Yu Jin transported the provisions in assistance and this enabled Zhang Liao to defeat Chen Lan and Mei Cheng. As a result, Cao Cao increased Yu Jin’s holdings to 200 households. This is a total of 1,200 households from previous rewards.
At that time, Yu Jin together with Zhang Liao, Yue Jin, Zhang He and Xu Huang were all of Cao Cao’s famous generals. Each time Cao Cao set out on his campaigns, they will become the vanguard and when retreating, they are the rearguard. Yu Jin is extremely strict when it comes to military discipline. Every time he obtains the valuables of an enemy, he will not hoard it for himself. As a result, Yu Jin’s rewards were extremely favourable. But as he was too strict, he could not entirely win the heart of his men.
Cao Cao often bore a grudge against Zhu Ling and he intended to stripped the latter’s military powers. As Zhu Ling was in awe of Yu Jin’s reputation, he did not dare to revolt. Thus, Cao Cao assigned Zhu Ling to serve under Yu Jin. All respected the decision. This clearly shows the awe in which Yu Jin was held by people in general. Cao Cao then promoted Yu Jin to the General of the Left and rewarded him with a ceremonial axe and assigned 500 households to him. One of Yu Jin’s sons was enfeoffed as a ranking marquis.
In the 24th year of Jian An (AD 219), Cao Cao, who was at Chang An at the time, sent Cao Ren on a punitive expedition against Guan Yu at Fan castle. He sent Yu Jin to assist Cao Ren. There was heavy rain during the autumn season for that year and Han Shui overflowed. The level terrain was flooded by more than a fewzhang(onezhangis about 3 1/3 meters) high. Yu Jin and the other troops climbed to high ground to monitor the flood and there were no place to escape. Guan Yu later attacked them in a huge boat. Yu Jin was unable to prevail and he surrendered. Only Pang De refused to surrender and was killed.
When Cao Cao received news about the matter, he sighed for a long time and said, “I have used Yu Jin for thirty years and I never expect him to surrender at the last moment and betraying his honour. He is unable to equal Pang De.”
At the same time, Sun Quan captured Guan Yu and his subordinates. Yu Jin surrendered to Wu. When Cao Pi ascended the throne, Sun Quan submitted as his vassal and he sent Yu Jin back. Wen Di (Cao Pi) gave an audience to Yu Jin. His hair has turned white and he looked very exhausted. When Yu Jin saw Wen Di, he collapsed and wept. Cao Pi consoled him and used the example of Xun Ling Fu and Meng Ming Shi as a precedent (meaning that the defeat at Fan castle was caused by the flood and not Yu Jin’s fault) and pardoned Yu Jin. He appointed Yu Jin as the General who Pacifies Far-Off Lands and sent him as an emissary to Wu. He (Cao Pi) first ordered Yu Jin to pay his respects at Cao Cao’s mausoleum at Ye. Cao Pi had earlier sent workmen to paint on the walls of the mausoleum showing the victory of Guan Yu with Pang De in anger and Yu Jin surrendering. When Yu Jin saw the drawing, he felt extremely shameful, then fell sick and died. His son, Yu Gui, succeeded his position and was appointed as the Marquis of Continuous Longevity. Yu Jin was posthumously appointed as the Marquis of Severity.
